g.data-narration: Welcome to this lecture on Shared Libraries in Linux Unix Systems. Let's explore this essential concept together.
Shared libraries are files containing compiled code that multiple programs can use at runtime, reducing redundancy and improving efficiency.
g.text: Shared libraries (.so files in Linux) contain pre-compiled code that multiple programs
can load and use at runtime, enabling code sharing across processes.

g.data-narration: Shared libraries solve problems of static libraries by reducing executable size, saving memory, and allowing updates without recompilation.
g.text: Why Do We Need Shared Libraries?

g.data-narration: Linux shared libraries follow a specific naming convention: real name with version, symbolic link name for development, and major version for compatibility.
The dynamic linking process involves compilation, dynamic linking, runtime loading by ld-linux.so, and lazy symbol resolution through PLT and GOT.
g.text: Compiler generates object code with unresolved references
Linker creates executable with library references

g.data-narration: The dynamic linker searches for libraries in order: DT_RPATH (deprecated), LD_LIBRARY_PATH, cache file, and finally default system paths.
When a program runs, the kernel loads it, ld-linux.so loads libraries, and symbols are resolved using PLT and GOT tables for lazy binding.
Position Independent Code (PIC) allows library code to execute at any memory address, essential for sharing across processes and enabling ASLR security.
g.text: PIC is machine code that can execute correctly regardless of its absolute memory address.
This is essential for shared libraries because the same code must be shareable across
multiple processes at different memory addresses.

g.data-narration: Creating a shared library involves compiling with -fPIC flag, using -shared to create the .so file, and linking with -L and -l flags.
The dlopen API allows programs to load libraries at runtime without compile-time linking, using dlsym to find functions and dlclose to unload.
g.text: The dlopen API enables runtime library loading without compile-time linking:

g.data-narration: The ldd command displays shared library dependencies of an executable, showing library names and their load addresses.
g.text: The ldd command shows which shared libraries an executable depends on:

g.data-narration: The ldconfig command updates the library cache in /etc/ld.so.cache, enabling the system to find newly installed libraries.
g.text: After installing libraries in system directories, run ldconfig to update the cache:

g.data-narration: Shared libraries provide major benefits: reduced executable size, memory savings through code sharing, and easy updates without recompilation.
For exams, remember these key points: static vs shared, naming conventions, search order, -fPIC flag, and dlopen API functions.
g.text: Exam Tips - Key Points to Remember
Static (.a) linked at compile time; Shared (.so) linked at runtime
Naming: libname.so.major is real name, libname.so is link name
-fPIC flag is required for shared libraries; -shared creates .so file

g.data-narration: In this lecture, we covered shared libraries fundamentals, the linking process, search paths, PIC, and practical tools for working with dynamic libraries.
g.text: Shared libraries enable dynamic linking, code sharing, and runtime loading in Linux/Unix
Use ldd to inspect, dlopen to load dynamically, and ldconfig to manage system libraries
Practice creating and using shared libraries to master this essential concept!
